author: @jayceecee1
text: Lots of people are raising their eyebrows and saying they don't have these things in stock. TSP is also known as sugar soap. Easily available from diy shops. Boric acid and borax used to be easy to get, until it became common knowledge that it can be good for arthritis and many other health problems. Now the pharmaceutical industry has made it difficult and spread misinformation about it being dangerous. (Yes it is dangerous if you eat absolutely ridiculous amounts of it or put it undiluted in your eyes, otherwise safer than table salt) You can still get it if you try. Great cheap no toxic wood preserver too, Borax substitute won't work for either purpose. TSP on its own may be worth a try.

replies.author: @HawaiianHeartbreak
replies.text:
this is for brazing useing a pressurized flame ,with brazing rods with little to no tin and zinc added .not for soldering with a iron which cannot consistently saturate the work area with heats the are required to melt the flux ingredients ontop the area to be welded . soldering uses liquid low temperature flux to keep the area oxidation free till the solder cools down. 

if you do mean brazing but just mixed up words then i would suggest if possible picking the parts to be welded to clean them from the protective layer added to metals like copper . or sand it good .. 

but this method does work as long as the part remains free off oxidation till cool down

author: @jamesfisher4326
text: If you are using this flux remember that it can be quite corrosive.  Stainless steel gets it's corrosion resistance from forming a very thin coating of oxides on it's surface that protect the metal underneath.  This process is called passivation.  Soldering stainless requires a strong acid flux to depassivate the surface.  Using an abrasive also helps.  After using the flux the joint must be cleaned thoroughly then repassivated.  I use a solution of baking soda in water to do this.  The result is 50 year old solder joints that are still solid.

replies.author: @voldschen232
replies.text: most flux are actually corrosive some are just not as much corrosive than others, rubbing alcohol and baking soda with water are a good solution to neutralize the acid

replies.author: @Inertiatek
replies.text: Since it's acidic, it can't be used for electrical connections.  The flux will corrode the copper over time, producing an insulative 'cold solder joint'.  For pcbs, you have to use rosin flux.

author: @tookitogo
text: Just to be clear, DO NOT use this type of flux for electronics. It’s too aggressive and will corrode PCBs, stranded wire, etc.

author: @andrewbradford8474
text:
This reply contains information that validates Mr. Smith's flux recipe - please read for more information. 

The ratios of the ingredients specified were followed - of course using TABLESPOONS instead of TEASPOONS

2TSP - BORAX - eBay
3TSP - BORIC ACID -eBay
2TSP - TSP - eBay ( common cleaner found in home depot, Lowes)

The mixture was diluted with water in excess, and then brought to a boil until a gelatinous consistency was achieved, unlike the water paste shown in the video. 

A piece of copper was cleaned and then the jelly like mixture was smeared onto a clean area. Torch heat in excess of 1200 degrees F was applied, and the entire coated area remained the color of pure copper - while the surrounding area was subject to extreme oxidation. 

This flux recipe is a successful one from my tests, and I encourage all who want to make their own flux , to start with this video.

All ingredients are purchased from eBay for around $55 total, and can make you a batch of flux for $2.50, compared to the $10+ for a similar amount of commercially available flux. of course with the amounts you can obtain from eBay - you could have a lifetime supply of flux!

I encourage you to check the MSDS for commercial brazing fluxes, and you will discover the main ingredients of these fluxes - Boric acid, and Borax! 

Great video, Mr. Smith - I thank you for your assistance!

replies.author: @RustyShackleford1313
replies.text: Trisodium phosphate (tsp), it is a cleaning product found in the paint department in most US stores at least. There is also tsp substitutes which can be misleadingly labeled and will *not* work for this purpose. The ratio is 3:2:2 (boric acid:trisodium phosphate:borax). Also keep in mind this is an acidic flux which can be undesirable.

Simple and with out a lot of fuss and bother.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i know what you mean mate and i am afraid i don't know about using alsohol

replies.author: @pl5087
replies.text: Alcohol will be fine, but it might be tricky if you're using a brush to coat your flux. If you want to use alcohol, don't waste chemicals, just mix it with some borax and dip your piece into it. Then ignite the alcohol (take care not to ignite your flux dish ! if you do so, just cover it with a plate.). The flux should be evenly spread. But the flux on this video is better, because you can use it in a sprayer if you add more water to it.  (I always use tap water to make this flux, and it works just fine.)

author: @rhenway
text: would this be suitable for melting aluminum (aluminium) copper and bronze?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +George Wood it's just a borax flux mate

replies.author: @pl5087
replies.text: To melt copper and bronze, you just need to add some borax powder, that's all. This flux is for brazing and soldering purposes. Aluminium melts easily without any kind of flux, but problems might happen when it comes to soldering it with a torch. You'll need a special kind of solder, and a special kind of flux. This one will not work for soldering aluminium.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
sounds like the trisodium phosphate is the hardest of those three things to get since they are pulling anything with phosphorus in it off the shelves here, I had to use a cola (has phosphoric acid) to color anodize some titanium ages ago cause they sold nothing at a store here locally that had phosphorus in it including the TSP (all they had was TSP substitute free of phosphorus of course lol)

wonder how hard it would be to make TSP out of mono calcium phosphate fertilizer and say some drain cleaner (*sodium hydroxide) from what I am reading the monocalcium (*triple strength phosphorus fertilizer)  can be turned into phosphoric acid effectively by adding sulfuric acid to it and the acid and calcium react to make calcium sulfate which is supposed to be insoluble in water, so it separates out with nothing more than a fitration (if so) so I guess once you have phosphoric acid that could be added to the sodium hydroxide to get a sodium phosphate compound, just not sure what conditions to make sure it becomes TSP are required lol.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thomas Anderson TSP is pretty easy to buy mate - home depot sell it in bags - i got mine from ebay

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: I tried home despot here and they hardly sell anything it seems though I have not tried for TSP yet, will have to have a look at that for sure, everything else though like toluene and such they either had nothing or had some substitute like xylene instead only, most of the stuff I have found (which still excluded TSP)  was at ace hardware. could just be the part of the country I am in but being in an oil state I would have figured they would be ignoring most of the "save the planet suggestions the rest of the country is following. either way I would definitely like to find out how to make it from other compounds, since the making is often the fun anyway lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thomas Anderson absolutely mate and you learn tons from synthesizing your own stuff

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: also I guess it got missed here, that I said hardest, lol not hard, with some diligence you can get anything even in the most politically restrictive environment ha ha

author: @petermoll3421
text:
I was wondering what would be the most useful chemicals for the home expirimenter to have on hand.
We all don't have a full chemistry lab at our disposal.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: PETER MOLL It depends on what area you want to work in - there are a few basic reagents you need often like a range of solvents, acids and alkalis but it really is a matter of what you are interested in - the only reason i have a full chem stock is because when i want to demonstrate something i buy a lot of the chemical - like a kilo or so and then leave it in the stock cupboard so when it crops up again i have it. The area I work in means i have a lot of binders, solvents, acids and metal halides - but you will build up a stock of what is useful to you just by buying what you need when you need it
